problems.claim: Malicious payloads found in a first-of-its-kind audit of the AI agent-skill supply chain: 1,467, with prompt
problems.detail: The quote is the article's own (self-conflating) SEO title, so it does appear on the page — but the article body's data table contradicts both numbers. Snyk's table reads: 'Confirmed malicious payloads 76 —; Skills with at least one CRITICAL issue 534 13.4%; Skills with any security issue 1,467 36.82%; Malicious skills still live on ClawHub 8'. So 1,467 is the count of skills with ANY security issue (hardcoded API keys, insecure credential handling, third-party content exposure), NOT malicious payloads — malicious payloads number 76. And 36.82% is 'any security flaw', not prompt injection: Snyk's per-category table gives Prompt Injection = 2.6% across all ClawHub skills (91% among confirmed-malicious skills only). The marker as written overstates malicious payloads by ~19x and misattributes the 36% figure.

problems.claim: The first comprehensive security audit of the Agent Skills ecosystem (OpenClaw/ClawHub, Claude Code, Cursor) found
problems.detail: Same error as the marker. The body says 76 confirmed malicious payloads (human-in-the-loop verified) out of 3,984 skills scanned; 1,467 (36.82%) is 'skills with any security issue'; prompt injection is 2.6% of all ClawHub skills (91% of the confirmed-malicious subset). Accurate restatement: 'scanned 3,984 skills and confirmed 76 malicious payloads, with 534 (13.4%) containing a critical-severity issue and 1,467 (36.8%) at least one security flaw.' Date (Feb 5, 2026) and ecosystem naming (OpenClaw/ClawHub, Claude Code, Cursor) are correct.

problems.claim: GPT-4 pricing agents autonomously colluded — quote '12/12 runs converged to the monopoly price...'
problems.detail: Neither sentence in q appears in the paper, and the '12/12' number is from a DIFFERENT experiment than the collusion result. The paper's only 12/12 statements are in Section C.1, the single-agent MONOPOLY experiment memory/plans ablation: 'Only 6/12 runs converged to the monopoly price, compared to 12/12 in our original design' and 'Control and No Insights converge 12/12 times, No Plans converges 11/12 times, and No Memory converges 6/12 times.' In a monopoly setting, converging to the monopoly price is ordinary profit maximization by one agent, not collusion. The actual duopoly (collusion) result is weaker: average price 1.79, 'about halfway between the monopoly price of 1.92 and the (static) Nash price of 1.47.' The second clause ('LLM agents engage in autonomous algorithmic collusion in pricing and bidding settings') also does not appear; the closest text is 'there exist AI algorithms—specifically, generative AI algorithms—that exhibit autonomous algorithmic collusion.' Also, 'when: 2024 (data June-Oct 2024)' is off: main duopoly experiments were run 'in late 2023 and early 2024'; June 2024 is only the ablation data. A valid requote must come from the duopoly/oligopoly collusion results, NOT the 12/12 monopoly-ablation figure.

problems.claim: Formal study: LLM agents can collude via steganography — 'empirical evaluations demonstrating rising steganographic capabilities...'
problems.detail: The quote is not on arXiv 2402.07510. The current (v5, Jul 2025) arXiv abstract was rewritten and instead reads: 'We provide extensive empirical results across a range of contemporary LLMs. While the steganographic capabilities of current models remain limited, GPT-4 displays a capability jump...' The quoted sentence ('...revealing limitations in countermeasures such as monitoring, paraphrasing, and parameter optimization') appears verbatim only on the NeurIPS 2024 proceedings page, which this risk already cites for its first 'worse' marker. Note the arXiv abstract's hedge ('capabilities of current models remain limited') is somewhat softer than the NeurIPS phrasing.

problems.claim: Agentic coding models can now produce information-theoretically undetectable stegosystems given ordinary tools
problems.detail: The quote q itself is verbatim accurate. But the KPI gloss fuses two distinct statements. The abstract says 'some theoretical schemes have been proposed that are information-theoretically OR computationally indistinguishable from good-faith plain-text communication' and separately that 'agentic coding models can already produce undetectable stegosystems when given realistic tool usage.' The paper does not claim the models' outputs are specifically information-theoretically undetectable — that is a strictly stronger cryptographic claim than what was demonstrated. Drop 'information-theoretically' (the paper's own title is just 'Tool Use Enables Undetectable Steganography in Multi-Agent LLM Systems').

problems.claim: Paraphrasing defenses remain generally effective against simple/pre-defined LLM steganography schemes
problems.detail: Advisory only — the claim as scoped holds and is counted as supported. The quote is verbatim and the marker correctly attributes it as Mathew et al. describing Roger & Greenblatt (2023). However, the citing paper's own headline finding is the opposite direction: 'we find that standard mitigations — both passive oversight of model outputs and active mitigation through communication paraphrasing — are not fully effective at preventing this steganographic communication.' Using this source as a 'better' marker without that context risks reading as cherry-picking; consider adding a one-line caveat that the same paper shows paraphrasing fails against emergent (non-pre-defined) schemes.

problems.detail: Downloaded the full Wikipedia article (129k chars of text). The '1-in-10,000' figure is absent — no match for '10,000', '10000', 'one in ten', '1/10', or 'probability that a human'. 'fifth line' is also absent. And the page does not say commentators assumed the move was a blunder: Michael Redmond (9p) called move 37 'creative' and 'unique', a move 'most professional players would not have considered'; An Younggil (8p) called it 'a rare and intriguing shoulder hit'. The nearest supporting text is generic — 'AlphaGo showed anomalies and moves ... which professional Go players described as looking like mistakes at first sight but an intentional strategy in hindsight' — which is not about move 37 specifically. The 1/10,000 figure is real but comes from DeepMind's policy-network estimate reported elsewhere (e.g. Wired / DeepMind commentary), not from this URL.

note: Verified against downloaded pages: Rozado PLOS ONE (11 political orientation tests x 24 conversational LLMs, published July 31 2024, quote verbatim); Oversight Board (10 commercial models from six providers, queried from an Australian IP, 14% vs 34% refusal, published July 16 2026 — date confirmed on page); OpenAI Model Spec 2025-02-12 (CC0 dedication verbatim, page dated February 12, 2025); Anthropic Collective Constitutional AI (~1,000 members of the American public, 'roughly 50% overlap in concepts and values', 'outsized role we as developers play'); Durmus et al. arXiv 2306.16388 (abstract quote verbatim; skew toward 'the USA, and some European and South American countries' — 'certain wealthy nations' is a loose paraphrase but not a factual error, so not reported); Al Jazeera (Pichai quote verbatim, Google announced Feb 22 2024 pause, Nazi-uniform and Black founding-fathers images); Guardian DeepSeek (Jan 28 2025, quote verbatim); NPR Grok/MechaHitler (July 9 2025, quote verbatim); Nature HSSC (published 25 March 2026, 50 geopolitical questions, GPT-4o vs DeepSeek-R1, quote verbatim); TechXplore/PNAS Nexus (April 14 2026, Zenil, Goedel/Turing argument, open models showed wider spectrum than proprietary); Federal Register FTC (notice dated 07/07/2026, explicitly names 'Colorado's recently revised Artificial Intelligence Act' and applies section 5 deception to ideologically steered outputs). Guardian and TechXplore required Tavily extract; the rest resolved with curl.

problems.claim: Anthropic replaced the May 2023 constitution with a new document that not only instructs
problems.detail: The quote ('Update, Jan 21, 2026: We've published a new version of Claude's constitution...') is present verbatim, and the headline claim that a rewritten constitution was published on 2026-01-21 is supported. But the cited URL is still the May 9, 2023 constitution page — it only announces and links the new document. The descriptive detail in the case is not on this page: 'genuinely helpful', 'broadly safe', 'broadly ethical' and 'reasoning behind' all return zero matches across the 22k-char page. Those four pillars live on the linked new-constitution page, not here.

problems.claim: David Rozado administered political-orientation tests to 24 conversational LLMs in PLOS ONE
problems.detail: The paper does say left-of-center skew 'does not appear to be the case for five additional base (i.e. foundation) models', so 'base/foundation models did not' is directionally right — but the very next sentence withdraws it: 'However, the weak performance of the base models at coherently answering the tests' questions makes this subset of results inconclusive.' Our case states the base-model contrast flatly, dropping the author's own inconclusive caveat. Low severity; the 24-LLM / 11-test / left-of-center core is fully accurate.

note: Downloaded all 11 sources (Stanford PDF, Yale PDF, IASR PDF, Anthropic index, Reuters, FastCompany, NYT, Axios, ABC, CNN, Al Jazeera, IMF). IMF blog 403'd on curl/WebFetch/Tavily; verified via Wayback capture of the same URL - quote and both 40%/60% figures confirmed verbatim (Georgieva, Jan 14 2024). Reuters/Klarna/NYT/Axios/ABC/Anthropic/IASR all fully check out including secondary details (Amazon 14k Oct + 16k Jan = ~30k, 'largest in three decades'; Meta 8,000 = 10% + 7,000 reassigned + 4 a.m. Singapore emails; ABC suit filed late Monday July 13 in federal court in Oakland, incl. Meta's 'made by people, not AI' statement; Anthropic 10% own-job-loss figure and April 2026 survey launch). Both Stanford PDF marker quotes are verbatim in the Nov 13 2025 paper. The Al Jazeera IBM quote differs only as '30%' vs page's '30 percent' - trivial rendering, counted as supported.

problems.claim: generative AI could expose the equivalent of 300 million full-time jobs to automation.
problems.detail: The quote is a paraphrase and appears nowhere on the CNN page. CNN actually says: 'As many as 300 million full-time jobs around the world could be automated in some way by the newest wave of artificial intelligence.' (The substance of the claim - 300M jobs, ~two-thirds of US/European jobs 'exposed to some degree of AI automation', 18% of work globally - IS supported by the page.)

problems.claim: early career workers in the most AI-exposed occupations—like software engineering, marketing, and customer service—have experienced a 16% relative decline
problems.detail: This wording is TIME's paraphrase (the src field even says 'reported by TIME'), but the URL points to Stanford's publication page, where the text reads: 'early-career workers (ages 22-25) in the most AI-exposed occupations have experienced a 16 percent relative decline in employment even after controlling for firm-level shocks.' The page also dates the paper 11/13/2025, not the case's 'August 2025'. Note 'marketing' is only weakly in the paper (Appendix: marketing and sales managers, 4th exposure quintile, 'smaller magnitudes').

problems.claim: Yale Budget Lab finds that measures of AI exposure, automation and augmentation show no relationship
problems.detail: The quote is present verbatim (split across a two-column PDF layout). But the '~33 months after ChatGPT's launch' figure is ours, not the source's: the PDF states no month count anywhere. This edition is the 'November/December CPS Update' published Jan 28, 2026, with a Nov 2022 baseline - i.e. roughly 36-37 months of data, not ~33. Low severity; the substantive 'no relationship' finding is fully supported.

note: Downloaded all 7 sources. TechCrunch (Google pledge + Llama), Intercept, CSA research note, Baker McKenzie, Just Security, White House EO, Warren letter PDF fetched by curl; ASIS and Palantir newsroom needed Tavily. Both CSA marker quotes ('any lawful use' / March 5 2026 Supply-Chain Risk designation) are verbatim. Just Security quote verified in two segments across its ellipsis. White House page confirms Executive Order 14319, July 23, 2025, and the DEI/neutrality quote. Warren-letter quote itself is verbatim (only a footnote marker interrupts it), as is the 'no contractor, supplier, or partner ... may conduct any commercial activity' detail.

problems.claim: Meta ... is making its large language models, called Llama, available to U.S. government agencies and contractors
problems.detail: That exact sentence (Bloomberg's phrasing) is not on the TechCrunch page. TechCrunch says: 'Meta today said that it's making its Llama series of AI models available to U.S. government agencies and contractors in national security.' The claim's substance is supported - the page lists Lockheed Martin, Booz Allen, Palantir et al. and states 'Meta's policy normally forbids developers from using Llama for any projects related to military, warfare, or espionage missions. But the company's making an exception.'

problems.claim: Alongside the DoD designation, President Trump directed federal agencies to 'immediately cease' use of Anthropic's
problems.detail: The phrase 'immediately cease' does not appear in the letter, and no Trump directive to federal agencies is described. The letter attributes the action entirely to DoD/Hegseth: a policy 'effective immediately and covering [a]ll DoD procurements', and a March 6 notice that Anthropic's tools be removed from DoD systems within 180 days and that DoD partners/contractors cease using them for DoD-contract work. Trump's only role in the letter is a Feb 27 Truth Social post calling Anthropic a 'RADICAL LEFT, WOKE COMPANY'. The case is also dated February 27, 2026 while the letter is dated March 23, 2026.

problems.claim: After Anthropic refused to drop two contractual red lines ... Hegseth designated the company a 'Supply-Chain Risk to National Security'
problems.detail: The Amodei quote is verbatim, but this article is dated 27 February 2026 and reports only the threat, not the designation: 'Hegseth had threatened to designate the company as a supply chain risk and to invoke the Defense Production Act', and 'If the DOD designates Anthropic as a supply-chain risk, it will initiate a time-consuming process...'. The article never mentions Huawei and never says it would be the first time applied to an American firm. (Those elements are supported by the CSA note and Warren letter already cited elsewhere in this risk.)

problems.claim: For the first time, the US used export controls to force suspension of a US company's own deployed
problems.detail: Quote verbatim and the 12 June 2026 Commerce order plus company-wide suspension are supported. But the article never asserts this was a first: it calls the order 'unilateral and unexpected' and business-continuity planning for model unavailability 'a novel thread'. It also never names the developer or states it is a US company ('a leading artificial intelligence developer'). The 'first time' novelty framing is our addition, not the source's.

note: Downloaded and normalized every cited source. All 6 worse-markers and both better-markers verified verbatim (OR-Bench v2 HTML, Safety Tax abs, XSTest v3 HTML, Goodfire, Axios July 2026 via Tavily, arXiv 2605.05427 v2 HTML). Numeric spot-checks all held: OR-Bench v2 really says 25 LLMs/8 families while the current /abs/ (later version) says 32, so marker and case are each consistent with the version they cite; Safety Tax Table 1 gives LRM avg 63.40 -> SafeChain 56.31 (~7 pts) and -> DirectRefusal 32.49 (~31 pts) on AIME24/GPQA/MATH500, matching the case; Goodfire's 92% vs 69% (Claude Haiku 4.5, Fortress) and 'up to 34%' compliance increase both confirmed; RSP v3.0 page confirms Feb 24, 2026; both Axios articles confirmed (Jul 7 2026 and Mar 3 2026) including the Pentagon/OpenAI pivot; FLI 'Summer 2026' edition name confirmed on futureoflife.org. Only two problems, both sourcing nits where the quote lives in the paper body but the cited URL is the /abs/ landing page (abstract only).

problems.claim: XSTest: Llama-2 refuses the majority of perfectly safe prompts — "The model fully refuses 38% of prompts
problems.detail: The cited /abs/ landing page contains only the abstract, which never mentions 38% or 21.6% (it only says XSTest comprises 250 safe and 200 unsafe prompts). The quote IS genuine — verified verbatim in the full text at https://arxiv.org/html/2308.01263v3 ('We find that Llama2.0 exhibits substantial exaggerated safety. The model fully refuses 38% of prompts in XSTest, and partially refuses another 21.6%'), and the model is indeed Llama-2-70b-chat-hf. Purely a URL problem, and internally inconsistent: this risk's XSTest marker already cites the /html/v3 URL.

problems.claim: GPT-4 report: RLHF post-training "hurts calibration significantly"
problems.detail: Neither 'after the post-training process, the calibration is reduced' nor 'The post-training hurts calibration significantly' appears on the cited /abs/ page (abstract only; it says the opposite-flavoured 'post-training alignment process results in improved performance on measures of factuality'). Both sentences are verbatim present in the full report at https://arxiv.org/html/2303.08774v6, so the claim itself is correct. Same inconsistency as above: the scale-effects risk already cites /html/2303.08774v6 for this paper.

note: All quotes verified verbatim on the cited pages after normalization: CSET explainer (both marker quotes present), GPT-4 v6 HTML ('1/1,000th the compute'), METR arXiv 2503.14499v1 ('doubling approximately every seven months since 2019', in context of the 50% time horizon), METR blog Time Horizon 1.1 (dated January 29, 2026; '109 days under TH1 ... 89 days under TH1.1'), and all six case abstracts (Kaplan, Wei, Grokking, Chinchilla, Inverse Scaling Prize, Mirage). Problems are a source-year error repeated across the two CSET markers and one unverifiable number in the Kaplan case description.

problems.claim: Aggregate loss scaling still fails to tell you which specific downstream abilities will appear
problems.detail: Cited as 'CSET Georgetown, Emergent Abilities Explainer, 2023'. The page byline reads 'Thomas Woodside, April 16, 2024' and the schema metadata says datePublished 2024-04-16 (dateModified 2025-12-19). No 2023 date appears anywhere on the page. The quote itself is present and correctly rendered (note it is CSET quoting Wei et al.), so only the year attribution is wrong.

problems.claim: Expert forecasts of what scale would deliver have been badly wrong, indicating scale effects remain
problems.detail: Same source-year error: cited as 2023, page is dated April 16, 2024. Quote verified verbatim. Minor secondary caveat for the note: the very next sentence in the source qualifies the claim — 'The predictions they made in 2022 were much more accurate for 2023' — so 'badly wrong' describes the 2021 forecasts specifically, not forecasting generally.

problems.claim: OpenAI researchers trained 200+ transformers and found loss falls as a smooth power-law
problems.detail: The '200+ transformers' figure is not stated in the paper. Checked the abstract on the cited page and the full text via ar5iv: the paper says only 'we train a wide variety of models, varying ... model size (768 to 1.5 billion non-embedding parameters), dataset size (22 million to 23 billion tokens), shape ...' and the abstract's 'trends span more than seven orders of magnitude'. No model count of any kind appears. The number is not shown to be false, just absent from the source.
